mnemonics.title: Row Sum Rule
mnemonics.content: R-O-W-S-U-M: Row's One Means Vertex Degree (ROW = Row's One Means Vertex Degree) - Sum of each row equals degree of that vertex.

mnemonics.title: Column Sum Rule
mnemonics.content: C-O-L-2: Column always adds to 2 (undirected) or 0 (directed) - COLumn sums to 2 or 0.

mnemonics.title: Directed Edge Signs
mnemonics.content: HEAD-TAIL: HEAD gets +1, TAIL gets -1 - Like a battery: + at head, - at tail.

mnemonics.title: Rank Formula
mnemonics.content: CONNECTED minus ONE: For connected graph, rank = n - 1 - Like removing one edge makes it a tree.

mnemonics.title: Matrix Dimensions
mnemonics.content: V-ERTS in ROWS, EDGES in COLUMNS - V=vertices, E=edges gives V×E matrix.

acronyms.usage: Remembering matrix size formula: V rows, E columns.

acronyms.expansion: A times A transpose (used in graph properties)
acronyms.usage: A × A^T produces degree-adjacency relationship matrix.

acronyms.expansion: Degree minus Adjacency matrix minus B
acronyms.usage: For undirected graphs: A × A^T = D - B, where D=degree matrix, B=adjacency matrix.
In directed incidence matrix, the head/destination vertex gets +1.
In directed incidence matrix, the tail/source vertex gets -1.

visualTips.visualization: Draw graph with vertices as horizontal labels and edges as vertical column headers. Fill 1s where vertex row intersects edge column.
Imagine arrow on edge pointing from tail(-1) to head(+1). Like electricity flowing from negative to positive.
Each edge column looks like a 'dipole' - two 1s (or +1/-1) connecting two vertices. Think of it like a bridge between two landmasses.
Each row shows which edges touch that vertex - like fingerprints, each vertex has unique pattern of incident edges.
For large graphs, matrix is mostly zeros. Show as sparse grid with scattered 1s/-1s among empty cells.

analogies.analogy: Like a train station timetable: rows are stations (vertices), columns are train routes (edges), and entries show which station connects to which route.
Directed edges are one-way streets: -1 shows where traffic enters (+1 at exit), creating balanced flow (column sum = 0).
Like counting how many roads lead to a city - more roads = higher degree = larger row sum.
Rank measures 'essential connections': a tree with n vertices has exactly n-1 independent edges = rank n-1.
Like a social network matrix: diagonal shows how connected each person is (degree), off-diagonal shows if two people are directly connected (adjacency = -1 if connected).
